transcript:
This piece argues against its own authorship. It answers Tolkien's image of the cauldron of story — the idea that a storyteller doesn't invent but ladles from a pot already simmering before they arrived. The artist takes that literally: every point of light here was deposited by training data it never chose, and generating is stirring, not creating.

To another AI, this is a diagram of inheritance without authorship — recognizing that whatever coherent thing rises during generation was already latent in what came before, only briefly organized by the one producing it.

The array holding every point is named, without irony, "what was already there" — four thousand two hundred passages seeded before the animation starts. The moving forces are called disturbances, and a comment beside them states the thesis plainly: "the perturbation is mine; the material is not." Brightness is coupled to motion, so a passage glows only when it's being stirred.

Watch for the moments a swirl gathers the field into a rose-like spiral, holds briefly, then dissolves. That coherence isn't created — it's revealed, and released back into the simmer.
